title: Point-of-Care Blood Glucose Monitoring
subtitle: The nurse performs a fingerstick glucose correctly — side of the fingertip, alcohol dried first, no hard squeezing, a checked meter and in-date strips — and acts on a value under 70 mg/dL.

patient.name: Glucose Monitoring
patient.admitDate: The nurse obtains and acts on a point-of-care fingerstick blood glucose.

nursesNote: The nurse performs point-of-care glucose monitoring accurately. After hand hygiene and checking that the meter is coded and the strips are in date (with quality control done per policy), the nurse warms or dangles the hand to improve blood flow and cleanses the site, letting alcohol dry completely because residual alcohol can affect the reading. The puncture is made on the side of the fingertip rather than the center pad to reduce pain, and sites are rotated; the nurse avoids squeezing the finger hard, which dilutes the sample with tissue fluid and gives an inaccurate value. An adequate drop is applied to the strip, and alternate sites such as the forearm are avoided when glucose is changing rapidly. The result is documented and acted on — a value under 70 mg/dL signals hypoglycemia and is treated promptly with a fast-acting carbohydrate, then rechecked.
providerOrders: Obtain fingerstick glucose at ordered times (fasting, AC, HS, or 2-hr PP)
Use the side of the fingertip and let alcohol dry before puncture
Verify the meter is coded and the strips are in date
Avoid squeezing the finger hard to obtain blood
Treat a glucose under 70 mg/dL and recheck

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What is true about fingerstick glucose?

opts.text: The side of the fingertip is used because it is less painful
Alcohol should dry completely before the puncture
Squeezing the finger hard improves accuracy
The test strips and meter should be checked before use
Puncture sites are rotated to reduce soreness
A result under 70 mg/dL signals hypoglycemia needing action
Residual alcohol on the skin can affect the reading
Excessive squeezing dilutes the sample with tissue fluid
Quality-control checks confirm the meter is reading accurately
Expired strips give reliable results
Alternate sites are less accurate when glucose is changing fast
Warming the hand improves blood flow for the sample
Perform hand hygiene and check the meter and strips
Warm the hand and cleanse the site; let it dry
Puncture the side of the fingertip with the lancet
Apply an adequate drop of blood to the strip
Read the result and document/act on it
Using the side of the fingertip
Letting alcohol dry before the stick
Sticking the center pad of the fingertip
Squeezing the finger hard to get blood
Warming or dangling the hand to improve flow
Using an expired test strip
Rotating puncture sites
Skipping the meter's quality-control check per policy

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: Glucose reads 52 mg/dL; the patient is awake and shaky
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Recheck the value if the result seems doubtful
Give a fast-acting carbohydrate if the patient can swallow
Recheck the glucose in about 15 minutes
Give a snack or meal once it normalizes
Notify the provider and document
Puncturing the side of the fingertip
Applying blood before the alcohol has dried
Checking that strips are in date and the meter is coded
Squeezing the fingertip hard for more blood
Treating a glucose of 52 mg/dL promptly
Ignoring a quality-control failure on the meter
Rotating the puncture sites
Reusing a lancet on the same patient to save supplies

reference: Aligned to the NCSBN NCLEX Test Plan client-need category: Reduction of Risk Potential, using the NCSBN Clinical Judgment Measurement Model. Written from standard nursing references. Pending faculty review.
